message.content:
Research the latest recommended way to set up Storybook for React Native in 2025-2026. I need:

1. What Storybook version works with React Native 0.73.x?
2. The exact packages needed (devDependencies)
3. The minimal configuration files needed
4. Whether @storybook/react-native-web is a viable approach for running in browser (not on device)
5. Key gotchas for React Native Storybook setup

Context: This is a React Native 0.73.6 project with react 18.2.0. We want `npm run storybook` to open in a browser for quick visual validation. We do NOT need on-device Storybook.

Provide a concrete, minimal setup recipe.

content.text: I'll research the current state of React Native Storybook setup for 2025-2026, focusing on browser-based workflows.

input.query: Storybook React Native web browser setup 2026

Based on the web search results, here's what I found about Storybook React Native 0.73 setup:

## Current State of Storybook for React Native

The latest version documented is v10, with v9 docs available separately. The search results don't show specific information about React Native version 0.73, but they do provide current setup guidance.

## Setup Instructions

### Quick Start with Expo Template
For Expo, you can use a template with the command: `npx create-expo-app --template expo-template-storybook AwesomeStorybook`

### Metro Configuration
You need to wrap your config in the withStorybook function in your metro.config.js file. The configuration looks like:

```javascript
const { getDefaultConfig } = require('expo/metro-config');
const { withStorybook } = require('@storybook/react-native/metro/withStorybook');
const config = getDefaultConfig(__dirname);
module.exports = withStorybook(config, {
  enabled: process.env.EXPO_PUBLIC_STORYBOOK_ENABLED === 'true',
  configPath: './.rnstorybook'
});
```

## Important Notes

You need to align your Storybook dependencies to the same major version or you will see broken behavior. The setup requires the unstable_allowRequireContext transformer option to enable dynamic story imports.

For more detailed information, a new docs site is being built for Storybook for React Native at https://storybookjs.github.io/react-native/docs/intro/.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

Based on the search results, here's what I found regarding @storybook/react-native version 7 and 8 compatibility:

## Version 7 & 8 Status

React Native Storybook 7 is based on the core entirely, which means that RN Storybook will inherit core improvements as they become available, and makes it easier for RN Storybook to keep up with core in the future.

React Native Storybook 7 aligns RN Storybook to core Storybook 7.6, enhances code re-use between @storybook/react and @storybook/react-native, and builds the foundations for more frequent and streamlined future releases.

## Storybook 8 Status

Regarding version 8 compatibility: Work on RN Storybook 8 is already underway. The basics are functional, but we have plans for many more improvements in the months ahead.

The search results from February 2024 indicate that Storybook 8 was in development. However, more recent information would be needed to determine the current state of @storybook/react-native version 10 (which appears to be the latest version as of February 2026).

## Important Note on Version Alignment

Make sure you align your storybook dependencies to the same major version or you will see broken behaviour.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

I've found comprehensive information about setting up Storybook for React Native with web browser support. Here are the key findings:

## Current Approach (2026)

Storybook for React Native Web is a framework that makes it easy to develop and test UI components in isolation for React Native, using Vite to build your components for web browsers.

## Installation Methods

The easiest way to use React Native and React Native Web is to select the "Both" option when installing Storybook, which will install and create configurations for both environments, allowing you to run Storybook for both in the same project.

You can add a React Native Web Storybook alongside an existing React Native Storybook by running the install command and selecting "React Native Web" in the setup wizard.

## Framework Requirements

This framework is designed to work with Storybook 8.5 and above for the best experience, and support cannot be provided if you're using an older Storybook version.

## Key Dependencies

For a typical setup, you would need to install packages like:
- Storybook packages, react-native-web, @storybook/addon-react-native-web, babel-plugin-react-native-web, @react-native/babel-preset, and webpack

## Web vs. Native Options

The Web option displays your React Native components in the browser and is based on Storybook for Web, which is feature-rich and mature. This provides benefits like documentation, testing, and access to 500+ addons.

The search results provide current documentation from official Storybook sources and recent tutorials for setting up Storybook with React Native web support.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

Based on the search results, here's what I found regarding @storybook/react-native and React Native 0.73 compatibility:

Storybook requires React Native 0.72+, which means React Native 0.73 should be compatible with @storybook/react-native. The search results indicate that the require.context feature was added in React Native 0.72, and with require.context in RN Storybook 7, you only need to run generation when you update the stories field of main.ts.

The most recent version of @storybook/react-native is version 10. Version 10 brings Storybook React Native in sync with Storybook core v10 and introduces improved Metro configuration. Since React Native 0.73 meets the minimum version requirement of 0.72+, it should be compatible with current versions of @storybook/react-native.

However, the search results don't provide explicit version-specific compatibility information between @storybook/react-native and React Native 0.73. For the most detailed and current compatibility information, I'd recommend checking the official npm package page for @storybook/react-native or the GitHub repository's release notes and documentation.

Based on the search results, here's what I found about Storybook React Native Web with Vite minimal setup and devDependencies:

## Key Information

Storybook for React Native Web is a framework that makes it easy to develop and test UI components in isolation for React Native, using Vite to build your components for web browsers.

### Minimal Setup devDependencies

For a minimal setup, when installing Storybook with the React Native Web framework, the installation process offers the option to choose either the minimal development option or the recommended option that includes testing tools.

According to recent installation logs from February 2026, when selecting the recommended configuration, the devDependencies added include: storybook@^10.2.7, @storybook/react-native-web-vite@^10.2.7, @chromatic-com/storybook@^5.0.1, @storybook/addon-vitest@^10.2.7, @storybook/addon-a11y@^10.2.7, @storybook/addon-docs@^10.2.7, vite@^7.3.1, eslint-plugin-storybook@^10.2.7, vitest, playwright, and related packages.

### Framework Configuration

This framework is designed to work with Storybook 8.5 and above for the best experience, and support cannot be provided for older Storybook versions.

For configuration details, the framework uses `@storybook/react-native-web-vite` and allows customization through the `.storybook/main.ts` file with options for transpiling modules, Babel configuration, and React plugin options.

Based on the search results, here are some key gotchas, issues, and common problems with Storybook React Native:

## Installation and Configuration Issues

When installing Storybook for React Native Web, selecting the recommended option that includes testing tools can result in a broken installation with references to testing addons that were not properly installed.

Configuration generators may not properly generate configuration that works with React Native components, instead generating Vite configuration and vanilla React setup when a Metro configuration is expected.

## Import and Bundling Problems

The @storybook/test import in stories fails the bundling process for web and native when starting up storybook for react-native. This is particularly problematic when trying to share stories between web and React Native implementations.

Issues with "typeof" imports from react-native libraries can occur during bundling, which may require adding aliases in the Vite config.

## Dependency and Addon Compatibility

It's important to align your storybook dependencies to the same major version or you will see broken behaviour.

Setup has historically been tricky, with dependencies often not in sync and the overall experience not as smooth as for the web.

## Shared Story Challenges

Installing Storybook directly within the React Native project and mixing Storybook with app dependencies requires restarting the bundler often with cache resets, which slows down developers.

These issues suggest careful attention to version management, configuration setup, and architectural decisions when implementing Storybook for React Native projects.

input.query: React Native Web babel.config.js setup react-native-web alias 2026

content.content:
Web search results for query: "React Native Web babel.config.js setup react-native-web alias 2026"

Links: [{"title":"Setup // React Native for Web","url":"https://necolas.github.io/react-native-web/docs/setup/"},{"title":"babel-plugin-react-native-web - npm","url":"https://www.npmjs.com/package/babel-plugin-react-native-web"},{"title":"Multi-platform setup // React Native for Web","url":"https://necolas.github.io/react-native-web/docs/multi-platform/"},{"title":"Setting up aliases in React Native | by Enzo Perez | Medium","url":"https://medium.com/@enzzorp/setting-up-aliases-in-react-native-de6c73b0dc5f"},{"title":"Custom Aliases in React Native with Babel - Tim Novis","url":"https://www.novis.co/post/custom-aliases-in-react-native-with-babel/"},{"title":"react-native-web/packages/babel-plugin-react-native-web at master · necolas/react-native-web","url":"https://github.com/necolas/react-native-web/tree/master/packages/babel-plugin-react-native-web"},{"title":"Alias in React Native - DEV Community","url":"https://dev.to/suprabhasupi/alias-in-react-native-1oej"},{"title":"Troubleshooting babel.config.js Configuration Issues","url":"https://www.bacancytechnology.com/qanda/react-native/babel-config-js-configuration-issues"},{"title":"react native web with webpack configuration · GitHub","url":"https://gist.github.com/srikanthsunkari/64377454251739266ee5cd092209018e"},{"title":"Step 2: Setting up React with React-Native-Web - DEV Community","url":"https://dev.to/ugglr/step-2-setting-up-react-with-react-native-web-30ba"}]

Based on the search results, here's what I found about setting up React Native Web with babel.config.js and react-native-web alias:

## Babel Configuration for react-native-web Alias

Babel supports module aliasing using babel-plugin-module-resolver with a configuration like: `{ "plugins": [ ["module-resolver", { "alias": { "^react-native$": "react-native-web" } }] ] }`

## Using babel-plugin-react-native-web

For a more specialized approach, babel-plugin-react-native-web is a Babel plugin that will alias react-native to react-native-web and exclude any modules not required by your app (keeping bundle size down).

## General Alias Setup in babel.config.js

For custom aliases in your React Native project, you can use a pattern like this in your babel.config.js:

Using the module-resolver plugin with configuration including `root: ["./src"]` and defining aliases like `components: "./src/components"`, `screens: "./src/screens"`, `utils: "./src/utils"`

## Installation

To set up aliases, you'll want to install babel-plugin-module-resolver with yarn or npm.

The key is to install the appropriate Babel plugin and configure it in your babel.config.js file with the alias mapping for react-native to react-native-web.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.
